name: WFP Filter Creation Targeting EDR/AV Processes
description: Detects Windows Filtering Platform filter creation events (Event ID 5441) that reference known EDR or antivirus process names. Attackers use WFP to surgically block security software network communications without terminating the processes, evading traditional process-kill detections. This technique was popularized by tools such as SilentButDeadly and EDRSilencer.
query:
any where event.code == "5441" and (
  winlog.event_data.ApplicationPath : (
    "*SentinelAgent*", "*SentinelServiceHost*",
    "*CSFalconService*", "*CSFalconContainer*",
    "*MsMpEng*", "*MpCmdRun*", "*MsSense*",
    "*RepMgr*", "*RepWAC*", "*RepUtils*",
    "*CylanceSvc*", "*CylanceUI*",
    "*ccSvcHst*", "*Rtvscan*",
    "*McShield*", "*McAfeeFramework*",
    "*PccNTMon*", "*TMBMSRV*",
    "*SavService*", "*SAVAdminService*",
    "*avp.exe*", "*kavtray*",
    "*ekrn.exe*", "*egui.exe*",
    "*CortexXDR*", "*cyserver*",
    "*xagt.exe*", "*FireEyeAgent*",
    "*elastic-endpoint*", "*elastic-agent*"
  ) or
  message : (
    "*SentinelAgent*", "*CSFalconService*", "*MsMpEng*",
    "*RepMgr*", "*CylanceSvc*", "*ccSvcHst*",
    "*McShield*", "*PccNTMon*", "*SavService*",
    "*avp.exe*", "*ekrn.exe*", "*CortexXDR*",
    "*xagt.exe*", "*elastic-endpoint*"
  )
)
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
note:
## Investigation Steps

1. Identify the initiating process that created the WFP filter.
2. Determine which EDR/AV process was targeted by the filter condition.
3. Check for multiple filter additions in rapid succession (typical of automated tools).
4. Correlate with Event IDs 5157/5152 to confirm network blocking occurred.
5. Verify EDR cloud connectivity status on the affected endpoint.

## False Positives

- VPN software creating filters that incidentally reference security process paths.
- Legitimate security configuration management tools.
- EDR self-protection mechanisms creating their own WFP filters.

## Threshold Tuning

Consider creating a threshold rule requiring 3+ filter additions within 5 minutes to reduce noise.

name: WFP Blocked Outbound Connection from EDR/AV Process
description: Detects WFP blocking outbound network connections from known EDR or antivirus processes (Event IDs 5157, 5152). When an attacker uses WFP to isolate EDR agents from their cloud infrastructure, these blocked connection events are generated. This is a high-confidence indicator that security software network communications are being actively suppressed.
query:
any where event.code in ("5157", "5152") and
  winlog.event_data.Direction == "Outbound" and
  process.executable : (
    "*\\SentinelAgent.exe", "*\\SentinelServiceHost.exe",
    "*\\CSFalconService.exe", "*\\CSFalconContainer.exe",
    "*\\MsMpEng.exe", "*\\MpCmdRun.exe", "*\\MsSense.exe",
    "*\\RepMgr.exe", "*\\RepWAC.exe",
    "*\\CylanceSvc.exe",
    "*\\ccSvcHst.exe", "*\\Rtvscan.exe",
    "*\\McShield.exe", "*\\McAfeeFramework.exe",
    "*\\PccNTMon.exe", "*\\TMBMSRV.exe",
    "*\\SavService.exe", "*\\SAVAdminService.exe",
    "*\\avp.exe", "*\\kavtray.exe",
    "*\\ekrn.exe", "*\\egui.exe",
    "*\\CortexXDR.exe", "*\\cyserver.exe",
    "*\\xagt.exe", "*\\FireEyeAgent.exe",
    "*\\elastic-endpoint.exe", "*\\elastic-agent.exe"
  )
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
note:
## Investigation Steps

1. Determine which WFP filter is blocking the EDR process connections.
2. Identify the process that created the blocking filter (correlate with Event ID 5441).
3. Check if multiple EDR processes are being blocked simultaneously.
4. Assess the duration and scope of the network isolation.
5. Verify whether EDR telemetry gaps coincide with blocked connections.

## False Positives

- Temporary network outages may generate blocked connection events.
- Misconfigured firewall policies blocking security software.
- EDR update processes during maintenance windows.

name: Firewall Rule Created to Block EDR/AV Network Access
description: Detects creation of Windows Firewall rules via netsh.exe or PowerShell cmdlets that block outbound network traffic for known EDR and antivirus processes. Attackers may use this technique as an alternative to WFP filter manipulation to cut off security software from cloud telemetry and threat intelligence feeds.
query:
process where event.type == "start" and
  (process.name : ("netsh.exe", "powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe") or
   process.pe.original_file_name : ("netsh.exe", "PowerShell.EXE", "pwsh.dll")) and
  process.command_line : (
    "*advfirewall*block*SentinelAgent*",
    "*advfirewall*block*CSFalcon*",
    "*advfirewall*block*MsMpEng*",
    "*advfirewall*block*MpCmdRun*",
    "*advfirewall*block*MsSense*",
    "*advfirewall*block*RepMgr*",
    "*advfirewall*block*CylanceSvc*",
    "*advfirewall*block*ccSvcHst*",
    "*advfirewall*block*McShield*",
    "*advfirewall*block*PccNTMon*",
    "*advfirewall*block*SavService*",
    "*advfirewall*block*avp.exe*",
    "*advfirewall*block*ekrn*",
    "*advfirewall*block*CortexXDR*",
    "*advfirewall*block*xagt*",
    "*advfirewall*block*elastic-endpoint*",
    "*New-NetFirewallRule*Block*SentinelAgent*",
    "*New-NetFirewallRule*Block*CSFalcon*",
    "*New-NetFirewallRule*Block*MsMpEng*",
    "*New-NetFirewallRule*Block*elastic-endpoint*",
    "*New-NetFirewallRule*Block*CortexXDR*",
    "*New-NetFirewallRule*Block*CylanceSvc*"
  )
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
note:
## Investigation Steps

1. Identify the parent process that spawned netsh.exe or PowerShell.
2. Determine which EDR/AV process was targeted.
3. Check for additional firewall rule modifications in the same session.
4. Correlate with process creation events for the parent chain.
5. Verify whether the rule creation was part of authorized change management.

## False Positives

- IT administrators managing firewall rules via scripts.
- Group Policy-driven firewall configuration changes.

name: Suspicious Process Loading WFP Client Library
description: Detects non-standard processes loading fwpuclnt.dll (Windows Filtering Platform client library). Legitimate use of this DLL is limited to system services and firewall management tools. An untrusted process loading this library may indicate preparation for WFP filter manipulation to block EDR network communications.
query:
library where dll.name : "fwpuclnt.dll" and
  not process.executable : (
    "?:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe",
    "?:\\Windows\\System32\\lsass.exe",
    "?:\\Windows\\System32\\services.exe",
    "?:\\Windows\\System32\\mmc.exe",
    "?:\\Windows\\System32\\wbem\\WmiPrvSE.exe",
    "?:\\Windows\\System32\\netsh.exe",
    "?:\\Program Files\\Windows Defender\\*",
    "?:\\Program Files\\Windows Defender Advanced Threat Protection\\*",
    "?:\\Program Files (x86)\\*Sentinel*\\*",
    "?:\\Program Files\\*Sentinel*\\*",
    "?:\\Program Files\\CrowdStrike\\*",
    "?:\\Program Files (x86)\\CrowdStrike\\*"
  )
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
note:
## Investigation Steps

1. Identify the process loading fwpuclnt.dll and its origin.
2. Check the process digital signature and reputation.
3. Determine whether the process subsequently creates WFP filters.
4. Correlate with Event ID 5441 (WFP filter addition) events.
5. Check for EDR process enumeration preceding the DLL load.

## False Positives

- Third-party VPN clients that use WFP for traffic management.
- Network monitoring or packet capture tools.
- Security assessment tools during authorized testing.

## Tuning

Add trusted third-party applications to the exclusion list as needed.

name: Windows Defender Configuration Tampering via Registry
description: Detects unauthorized modifications to Windows Defender registry keys including Real-Time Protection, Cloud Protection (SpyNet), Exclusions, and Attack Surface Reduction rules. Attackers may modify these settings to weaken endpoint defenses before executing payloads or to complement WFP-based EDR network isolation attacks.
query:
registry where event.type == "change" and
  registry.path : (
    "*\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableRealtimeMonitoring",
    "*\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableBehaviorMonitoring",
    "*\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableOnAccessProtection",
    "*\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableScanOnRealtimeEnable",
    "*\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableIOAVProtection",
    "*\\Windows Defender\\SpyNet\\SpyNetReporting",
    "*\\Windows Defender\\SpyNet\\SubmitSamplesConsent",
    "*\\Windows Defender\\Exclusions\\*",
    "*\\Windows Defender\\Features\\TamperProtection",
    "*\\Windows Defender Exploit Guard\\ASR\\*"
  ) and
  not process.executable : (
    "?:\\Program Files\\Windows Defender\\MsMpEng.exe",
    "?:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe",
    "?:\\Windows\\System32\\gpupdate.exe",
    "?:\\Program Files\\Windows Defender Advanced Threat Protection\\*"
  )
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
note:
## Investigation Steps

1. Identify the process modifying Defender registry keys.
2. Check the process tree to find the root cause.
3. Determine which specific Defender feature was disabled.
4. Verify whether this was an authorized Group Policy change.
5. Check for complementary WFP filter creation or EDR process targeting.

## False Positives

- Group Policy updates modifying Defender settings.
- SCCM/Intune policy enforcement.
- Authorized IT administration scripts.

name: Enumeration of Multiple EDR/AV Processes
description: Detects process execution patterns that indicate enumeration of security software. This often serves as a reconnaissance step before WFP-based EDR network isolation or process termination attacks. The detection triggers when tasklist, wmic, or PowerShell commands query for multiple known EDR/AV process names.
query:
process where event.type == "start" and
  process.name : ("tasklist.exe", "wmic.exe", "powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe", "cmd.exe") and
  process.command_line : (
    "*SentinelAgent*CSFalcon*",
    "*SentinelAgent*MsMpEng*",
    "*CSFalcon*MsMpEng*",
    "*SentinelAgent*CortexXDR*",
    "*SentinelAgent*elastic-endpoint*",
    "*CSFalcon*CortexXDR*",
    "*MsMpEng*elastic-endpoint*",
    "*CylanceSvc*MsMpEng*",
    "*CylanceSvc*CSFalcon*"
  )
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
note:
## Investigation Steps

1. Identify the user account performing the enumeration.
2. Determine the parent process and execution context.
3. Check for subsequent WFP filter creation or firewall rule modification.
4. Correlate with other indicators of EDR evasion activity.
5. Assess whether the enumeration is part of legitimate IT inventory.

## False Positives

- IT asset inventory scripts querying running processes.
- System monitoring tools checking service health.
- Security assessment tools during authorized testing.

name: WFP-Based EDR Isolation Attack Sequence
description: Detects the full behavioral sequence of a WFP-based EDR isolation attack: a non-system process loading fwpuclnt.dll followed by WFP filter addition events targeting security software within a short time window. This sequence-based detection provides high confidence with minimal false positives.
query:
sequence by host.id with maxspan=2m
  [library where dll.name : "fwpuclnt.dll" and
    not process.executable : (
      "?:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe",
      "?:\\Windows\\System32\\lsass.exe",
      "?:\\Windows\\System32\\services.exe",
      "?:\\Windows\\System32\\netsh.exe",
      "?:\\Program Files\\Windows Defender\\*",
      "?:\\Program Files\\CrowdStrike\\*"
    )]
  [any where event.code == "5441" and
    message : (
      "*SentinelAgent*", "*CSFalcon*", "*MsMpEng*",
      "*RepMgr*", "*CylanceSvc*", "*ccSvcHst*",
      "*McShield*", "*avp.exe*", "*ekrn*",
      "*CortexXDR*", "*xagt*", "*elastic-endpoint*"
    )]
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
note:
## Investigation Steps

1. This is a HIGH CONFIDENCE detection - treat as a confirmed attack.
2. Immediately isolate the affected endpoint.
3. Identify the process that loaded fwpuclnt.dll and created WFP filters.
4. Determine the full process tree and initial access vector.
5. Check for lateral movement or additional payload delivery.
6. Collect memory dump for forensic analysis.

## False Positives

Very rare. This sequence-based detection requires both a suspicious DLL load and WFP filter creation targeting EDR processes within 2 minutes.
